drills.scenario: Maya runs a Shopify pet brand. Her developer says the Base Pixel Code is only worth installing on the checkout 'thank you' page because 'that's the only page where a sale happens, so that's all we need to track.'
drills.question: What should Maya tell the developer?
options: He's right — only the purchase page matters for tracking sales
Install the Base Pixel Code on every single page of the site, on page load
Only install it on the home page and the checkout page
Skip the pixel and rely on Shopify's own analytics
drills.explanation: The Base Pixel must fire on EVERY page, on page load. Tracking-only thinking underuses two of the pixel's Three Super Powers: Audience Building and Optimization. Site-wide visitor activity is the most common audience-building behavior, used to build retargeting audiences. Installing only on the purchase page throws away that data.

drills.scenario: A fitness coach sells a $54 USD recorded workshop. His Purchase event fires correctly, but in Ads Manager the purchase value column is blank and he can't calculate ROAS. His current event code is just: fbq('track', 'Purchase');
drills.question: What is the most likely cause and fix?
options: Purchase events never report a value in Ads Manager — this is normal
The Purchase event is missing its mandatory parameters; add value and currency
He needs to fire 'CompleteRegistration' instead of 'Purchase'
The Base Pixel ID is wrong on the thank-you page
drills.explanation: Purchase is the ONLY Standard Event with mandatory parameters: value and currency. Without them the event still registers but carries no revenue payload, so the value column stays blank. The fix is fbq('track', 'Purchase', { value: 54.00, currency: 'USD' });

drills.scenario: You are hand-coding pixel tracking for a webinar coach's landing page. You need the source code ordered so that the base pixel and a Lead event both fire correctly.
drills.question: Put these page-source elements in the correct top-to-bottom order.
options: Base Pixel Code placed in the <head>, firing on page load
fbq('init', 'PIXELID') initializes and associates activity with the ad account
fbq('track', 'PageView') registers the first event
Lead event code placed later in the <body>, after the base code
Lead event fires (e.g., on the opt-in form submission) using the already-loaded fbq
drills.explanation: Event code must come AFTER the Base Pixel Code in source order so fbq is loaded before any event fires. The base block goes in the <head> (init, then PageView), and event code like Lead lives later in the <body>. Placing event code before the base code means fbq isn't loaded yet and the event won't fire.

drills.scenario: A Shopify pet brand wants to retarget shoppers who showed buying intent but didn't complete the order. Their pixel correctly fires AddToCart and Purchase events with the right context.
drills.question: How should they build this retargeting audience?
options: Create a Custom Audience from Website Traffic > Custom Combination, include AddToCart and exclude Purchase
Create a Lookalike Audience from all website visitors
Create a Custom Audience by URL only, targeting the cart page URL
Lower the budget and wait for Facebook to find them automatically
drills.explanation: This is Audience Construction, one of the Three Ways Standard Events Are Used. In Audiences, create a Custom Audience based on Website Traffic, choose Custom Combination, switch the dropdown from URL to Event, then include people who fired AddToCart and exclude those who fired Purchase. A URL-only audience can't isolate event behavior, and a lookalike targets new prospects, not these warm cart abandoners.

drills.scenario: A local gym's developer fired the 'Purchase' Standard Event on the gym's 'Contact Us' page by mistake, thinking event names are enforced by Facebook. Now the reports look strange.
drills.question: What actually happened?
options: Facebook rejected the event because Purchase requires a real transaction
Facebook flagged the page and paused the account
Facebook recorded it as-is — event names carry no enforced meaning, so reporting is now confused
Nothing was recorded because Contact Us isn't a Standard Event page
drills.explanation: Aside from Purchase's mandatory parameters, Facebook doesn't know or care when/how you fire events — the names carry no special enforced meaning. Firing Purchase on a Contact Us page won't be flagged, but it pollutes reporting and audiences. Always fire events with the correct contextual meaning.

drills.scenario: A webinar coach wants the pixel to do real work, not just sit there. He asks which actions genuinely leverage the pixel's capabilities for his campaign.
drills.question: Which of these correctly leverage the pixel's Three Super Powers? (Select all that apply)
options: Fire CompleteRegistration when someone registers for the webinar, to track and report results
At the ad set level, set CompleteRegistration as the Optimization Event so Facebook finds more people likely to register
Build a Custom Audience of registrants who didn't attend, using event data
Install the Base Pixel only on the thank-you page to save load time
Treat the pixel as a tracking-only tag and ignore audiences and optimization
drills.explanation: The three super powers are Tracking & Measurement (report on CompleteRegistration), Optimization (set it as the ad set Optimization Event so the algorithm finds similar people), and Audience Building (build event-based Custom Audiences). Installing only on one page breaks audience building, and treating it as tracking-only underuses two of the three powers.

drills.scenario: The pet brand wants to retarget people who added a SPECIFIC product line ('Orthopedic Beds') to cart, not just any product. They're setting up the AddToCart event and a granular Custom Audience.
drills.question: What enables this product-level granularity?
options: Parameters — add a descriptor like product_name to the AddToCart event, then build the audience on that parameter
Nothing — AddToCart can't distinguish between products
A separate pixel ID for each product line
The mandatory value and currency parameters, which are required on AddToCart
drills.explanation: Parameters add an open-ended payload of descriptors to an event (e.g., product_name contains 'Orthopedic'). They can be used in Business Manager to build highly granular Custom Audiences or Custom Conversions. Note: parameters are only mandatory on Purchase; on AddToCart they're optional but enable this granularity. Also, only parameters that have already had site traffic appear in the pre-populated dropdowns.

drills.scenario: Before launching, the gym owner wants to verify the pixel fires correctly across the whole funnel (home > sales page > checkout > thank-you) using the Facebook Pixel Helper.
drills.question: Put the verification steps in the correct order.
options: Install the Facebook Pixel Helper Chrome extension
Click each ad's URL and confirm the pixel fires on every page of the funnel
Read the Pixel ID reported by the Helper on each page
Cross-reference that Pixel ID against your Ad Account Pixel ID in Ads Manager > Pixels
Confirm the IDs match on every page of the funnel
drills.explanation: Pixel verification: install the Pixel Helper, visit each funnel URL to confirm the pixel fires, read the reported Pixel ID, cross-reference it against your Ad Account Pixel ID in Ads Manager, and confirm the IDs match on every page. A mismatched or missing pixel anywhere in the funnel means broken tracking and attribution.
